summary: Gruber analyzes why News Corporation's iPad newspaper The Daily failed after less than two years. He rejects the popular narrative that tablet-only publishing is inherently doomed, arguing instead that The Daily failed for two simple reasons: the software was bad and the operation was absurdly overscaled. With $25 million in annual expenses but only around $4 million in subscription revenue, the math never worked. Gruber contends that a leaner team could have built a profitable daily news app on The Daily's 100,000-subscriber base, and that the real lesson is about execution, not platform strategy.
key_points: The Daily's app was slow, confusing, and poorly engineered from launch, squandering its massive initial publicity and Apple's endorsement
The Daily's $500,000/week operating costs were wildly disproportionate to what a daily iPad news app actually requires — Gruber estimates $5 million/year would suffice
News Corporation simply crammed a 20th-century newspaper model into an iPad wrapper rather than rethinking scope for digital
Felix Salmon's conclusion that tablet-native journalism is impossible confuses bad execution with platform limitations — all of The Daily's technical failures were solvable engineering problems
The Daily's 100,000 paying subscribers actually proved that iPad-only paid journalism can work; the failure was purely in cost structure
money_quotes: News Corporation went no further than taking the newspaper as we know it — the newspaper as defined by the pre-Internet 20th century — and cramming it into an iPad wrapper.
All impossible tasks have not been accomplished; but not all tasks that have not yet been accomplished are impossible.
The Daily's failure had nothing to do with it being iPad-only and everything to do with the fact that it just plain stunk.
They never seemed to treat software engineering and design as a primary function of the publication.
A news app needs to care about its software to the same high degree that print publications care about their paper, page design, and distribution.
You can't tell me a good daily iPad newspaper couldn't be run profitably for $5 million a year.
That most existing iPad magazine apps are slow, badly-designed, can't search, etc. does not mean iPad magazine apps cannot be fast, well-designed, and searchable.
The Daily's success proves the opposite of Salmon's conclusion: that an iPad-only daily news app could be a success.
key_insight: The Daily failed not because tablet-only publishing is impossible, but because it combined terrible software with a bloated cost structure inherited from legacy print thinking — proving that digital media requires engineering excellence and lean operations, not just content.
